title: Ponte Vecchio | Florence, History, & Facts | Britannica
highlights: Vecchio, the first segmental arch bridge built in the West, which crosses over the Arno River at Florence and is an outstanding engineering achievement of Europe’s Middle Ages. Its builder, Taddeo Gaddi, completed the bridge in 1345. Requiring fewer piers in the stream than the Roman semicircular-arch design, the segmental arch offered less obstruction to navigation and freer passage to floodwaters. The Ponte Vecchio roadway carries a two-story gallery, the upper gallery connecting the neighbouring Pitti, Uffizi, and other palaces and the lower gallery providing space for shops. During [

title: The Historical Evolution of Florence's Ponte Vecchio
highlights:
Vecchio, a medieval masterpiece that has evolved from a wooden  ...    In 1235, the bridge was rebuilt in stone, marking a significant turning point in its history. This sturdier version was designed to withstand the frequent floods that plagued Florence. Unfortunately, even this iteration of the Ponte Vecchio could not escape the devastating flood of 1333, which swept it away entirely. Only two of its central piers  ...    ---

## The Renaissance Rebirth The Ponte Vecchio we know today was reconstructed in 1345, and its design is widely attributed to Taddeo Gaddi, a renowned architect and painter of the time. This new version was revolutionary—it featured a series of shops lining both sides of the bridge, a concept that was rare in medieval Europe. These shops were originally occupied by butchers, fishmongers, and tanners, whose businesses thrived thanks to the proximity of the Arno River, which they used for  ... title: Ponte Vecchio - The (secret) old bridge in Florence
highlights: secret corridor between Palazzo Vecchio and Pitti in addition to numerous jewelers and shops.  ...    The most striking and oldest bridge in Florence Italy still in existence is the Ponte Vecchio, literally: the old bridge. The Ponte Vecchio was designed by a student of Giotto, Taddeo Gaddi and was finished in 1345. The Ponte Vecchio di Firenze is known for the many shops on and around the bridge, which used to be the domain of blacksmiths, tanners  ...    The Ponte Vecchio is said to have existed since Roman times. The bridge was then only made of wood and after the flooding of the bridge in 1333, the Ponte Vecchio Firenze was rebuilt in stone in 1345. The bridge consists of three arches, of which the middle one is the largest at thirty meters in length. title: The troubled past of Ponte Vecchio - Leonardo da Vinci Art School
highlights: history. In 1333 a terrible flood devastated the city of Florence and not even Ponte Vecchio was spared. It was destroyed by the water which carried away its entire structure, along with the large statue of the god Mars to whom the Romans had dedicated the city, leaving only two pillars standing. The reconstruction works on the "new" Ponte Vecchio began immediately after the flood and ended in 1345, under the guidance of Taddeo Gaddi, who helped create what turned out to be one of the most beautiful and avant-garde bridges in medieval Italy. Again, the merchants prepared to place their stalls along the bridge, but this time they were forbidden to place them  ...    Over the centuries, many more of these "suspended" shops were built, but they weren't quite the kind we see today.  ...
